threats.title: AccessControlHooks.onQueueWithdrawal Missing isHooked Check Allows Unauthorized Credential State Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: AccessControlHooks.onQueueWithdrawal does not validate that msg.sender is a registered hooked market (no `_hookedMarkets[msg.sender].isHooked` check), unlike onDeposit and onTransfer. Any external address can call this function, triggering `_tryValidateAccess` which writes to `_lenderStatus` storage and makes stateful external calls to role providers via `_tryValidateCredential`. An attacker can call `onQueueWithdrawal(victim, 0, 0, fakeState, craftedHooksData)` to manipulate any lender's credential state. When `_tryValidateAccess` returns true (valid credential found via hooksData), the function completes successfully, persisting storage writes including provider state changes from `validateCredential` calls.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a missing access control check on a state-changing function. FixedTermLoanHooks correctly includes `if (!market.isHooked) revert NotHookedMarket();` at the start of onQueueWithdrawal, but AccessControlHooks omits it entirely. This allows any caller to trigger credential validation flows and persist storage changes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing `HookedMarket memory market = _hookedMarkets[msg.sender]; if (!market.isHooked) revert NotHookedMarket();` that exists in onDeposit (same file) and in FixedTermLoanHooks.onQueueWithdrawal

preconditions: Target lender must exist in _lenderStatus (has or had a credential)
Attacker provides hooksData with >20 bytes pointing to a valid role provider for validateCredential path, or lender has expired credential that gets cleared

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: onQueueWithdrawal in AccessControlHooks has no msg.sender validation, while all other state-changing hooks (onDeposit, onTransfer) do
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - any caller can exploit this
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - available whenever a lender has any credential state

operational_questions: Can an attacker consume one-time-use credentials on providers by calling validateCredential through this path?
Does clearing an expired credential prevent the lender from refreshing it through normal market interaction?
Can the attacker trigger unintended state changes in role providers via validateCredential?
validation_requirements: Call onQueueWithdrawal from non-market address and verify storage is modified
Confirm _tryValidateCredential makes CALL (not staticcall) to provider, enabling stateful side effects
Test with various hooksData payloads to enumerate exploitation paths

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify a lender with an active or expired credential in _lenderStatus
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Craft hooksData with >20 bytes pointing to a registered role provider
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call onQueueWithdrawal(victimAddress, 0, 0, fakeState, craftedHooksData) from attacker EOA
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If validateCredential succeeds, lender's credential state is updated and provider's one-time credential is consumed without an actual withdrawal

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Credential state for any lender can be manipulated by unauthorized callers
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Lenders may lose one-time credentials, need re-authentication, or have credential state unexpectedly modified
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Lender must re-obtain credentials from role providers
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All credential-gated operations (deposit, transfer, withdrawal) affected indirectly

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic - craft calldata to a public function

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Calls to onQueueWithdrawal from non-market addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected credential state changes without corresponding market interactions
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated calls targeting specific lender addresses

threats.title: HooksConfig.onSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips Checks returndatasize Before External Call Due to Yul Evaluation Order
threats.hypothesis_description: In HooksConfig.sol's `onSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips`, the assembly block uses `if or(lt(returndatasize(), 0x40), iszero(call(gas(), target, ...)))`. Yul evaluates arguments left-to-right, so `returndatasize()` is read BEFORE the `call` is made. This means `lt(returndatasize(), 0x40)` checks the return data size from the PREVIOUS external call in the market's execution context, not from the hooks call. If no prior call returned >= 64 bytes (or no prior call was made), this evaluates to true, making the `or` always true, causing a revert even when the hooks call succeeds and returns valid data. This could brick the `setAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips` functionality entirely.
threats.investigation_rationale: The developer's intent was to check both call failure AND insufficient return data. But Yul left-to-right evaluation means the returndatasize check uses stale data. Other hook functions (onDeposit, onTransfer, etc.) don't have this pattern since they return void. This is the only hook that expects return data.

proof_of_signal.pattern: or(lt(returndatasize(), 0x40), iszero(call(...))) evaluates returndatasize() before the call opcode executes; should be or(iszero(call(...)), lt(returndatasize(), 0x40)) or separate checks

preconditions: The market contract's execution path for setAnnualInterestBips must not include a prior external call returning >= 64 bytes before invoking this library function
The hooks flag useOnSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips must be enabled

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Yul evaluates `lt(returndatasize(), 0x40)` BEFORE the `call` opcode, checking stale RETURNDATASIZE from prior execution context
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Affects borrower's ability to adjust interest rates
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent if the market's execution path doesn't have a prior call with >= 64 bytes return data

operational_questions: What is the exact execution path in the market contract before calling this library function?
Does the market contract make any external calls returning >= 64 bytes before this point?
Can this be triggered deterministically to permanently brick interest rate changes?
validation_requirements: Deploy a market with hooks enabled for setAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips
Call setAnnualInterestBips and check if it reverts with the hooks return data
Verify by swapping or() operands that the bug is confirmed

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Analyze market's execution path to confirm no prior call returns >= 64 bytes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If confirmed, borrower cannot adjust interest rates, permanently locking parameters
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: No active exploitation needed - the bug manifests during normal operations
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - this is a latent code defect

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Borrower permanently unable to modify annual interest rate or reserve ratio through the hooks
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Market parameters frozen, borrower cannot respond to changing market conditions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No recovery possible without contract redeployment - market is non-upgradable
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: setAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips on all markets using this HooksConfig library

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time borrower tries to change interest rate

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Consistent failure to update interest rates on hooked markets
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: All attempts to change interest rates fail

threats.title: Unauthorized validateCredential Invocation Consumes One-Time Provider Credentials
threats.hypothesis_description: Building on the missing isHooked check in AccessControlHooks.onQueueWithdrawal (OPS-H001), an attacker can craft hooksData with >20 bytes to trigger `_tryValidateCredential`, which makes a stateful `CALL` (not staticcall) to the role provider's `validateCredential` function. If the provider implements one-time-use credential logic (consuming a token, incrementing a nonce, etc.), the attacker's unauthorized call burns the lender's credential without any actual market operation occurring. When the function succeeds (provider returns valid credential), storage is updated and the transaction completes, permanently consuming the credential.
threats.investigation_rationale: _tryValidateCredential uses `call(gas(), providerAddress, 0, ...)` which allows state changes in the provider. Combined with the missing isHooked check, anyone can trigger this stateful call for any lender address. The provider has no way to distinguish authorized market-originating calls from unauthorized attacker-originating calls.

proof_of_signal.pattern: call(gas(), providerAddress, 0, ...) is a stateful CALL, combined with missing isHooked check in onQueueWithdrawal allowing any caller to invoke this path

preconditions: A role provider with stateful validateCredential (one-time-use credentials) must be registered
Attacker knows the provider address and can craft valid hooksData format
Target lender must not be a known lender on the attacker's address (always true since attacker is not a market)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: CALL to external provider is made without verifying msg.sender is a legitimate market
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Anytime a lender has a one-time credential available

operational_questions: Which deployed role providers implement stateful validateCredential with one-time-use semantics?
Can providers distinguish between legitimate market calls and attacker calls?
What is the cost to the lender of having their credential consumed?
validation_requirements: Deploy a mock one-time credential provider
Call onQueueWithdrawal from attacker address with crafted hooksData
Verify provider's one-time credential is consumed

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify registered role providers with stateful validateCredential
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Provider's validateCredential is called, consuming the one-time credential. Lender must obtain a new credential.

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Lender credentials can be consumed by unauthorized parties
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Lenders denied access to deposits/withdrawals until new credentials obtained
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Lender must re-obtain credentials from the provider
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All credential-gated market operations for affected lenders

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After lender obtains credential but before they use it

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: validateCredential calls from non-market msg.sender contexts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Lender credentials disappearing without corresponding market operations

threats.title: Borrower Bypasses Temporary Reserve Ratio via Repeated 25% APR Step-Downs
threats.hypothesis_description: The temporary reserve ratio mechanism in MarketConstraintHooks allows a borrower to reduce APR by up to 25% (2500 bips relative difference) without triggering an elevated reserve ratio. After the 2-week temporary period expires, the new lower APR becomes the baseline for the next reduction. By repeating 25% reductions every 2 weeks, a borrower can reduce APR from 10000 to ~3164 bips (68% total reduction) over 8 weeks without ever triggering elevated reserve ratios above the original. This undermines the protection mechanism designed to give lenders time to exit.
threats.investigation_rationale: _calculateTemporaryReserveRatioBips returns originalReserveRatioBips when relativeDiff <= 2500. After expiry, delete temporaryExcessReserveRatio[market] resets the state, and the new current APR becomes the baseline for the next reduction. The mechanism has no memory of cumulative reductions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: relativeDiff <= 2500 returns originalReserveRatioBips unchanged; after expiry, delete temporaryExcessReserveRatio resets baseline to current lower APR

preconditions: Borrower controls setAnnualInterestBips on the market
Market has an existing APR high enough for multiple 25% reductions to be meaningful
Borrower is willing to wait 2 weeks between each reduction

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Each 25% reduction from the current baseline avoids elevated reserve ratio; mechanism has no cumulative reduction tracking

operational_questions: Is the 8-week timeline acceptable for lender protection, or should cumulative reductions be tracked?
Do lenders have sufficient off-chain notification to react within 2-week windows?
What is the economic impact of a 68% APR reduction over 8 weeks vs immediate?
validation_requirements: Simulate 4 sequential 25% APR reductions with 2-week waits
Verify each step triggers only the original reserve ratio
Calculate total APR reduction without elevated reserve protection

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Set APR to 75% of current (25% reduction) - no elevated reserve ratio
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait 2 weeks for temporary period to expire, APR rate becomes new baseline
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: After 8 weeks, APR reduced ~68% without ever triggering penalty reserve ratios

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Borrower effectively bypasses the reserve ratio protection mechanism
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Lenders receive drastically reduced yield without proportional reserve protection
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No mechanism for lenders to prevent gradual reduction

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - single borrower acting over time

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Monotonically decreasing APR with no elevated reserve ratio events

threats.title: Reentrancy via Stateful CALL in _tryValidateCredential Enables Provider-Driven State Corruption
threats.hypothesis_description: _tryValidateCredential uses `call(gas(), providerAddress, ...)` (a stateful CALL, not staticcall) to invoke validateCredential on external role providers. This occurs within onDeposit, onTransfer, and onQueueWithdrawal hooks before the lender's credential state is finalized in storage. A malicious or compromised role provider can use this callback to reenter the hooks contract or the calling market contract, potentially manipulating state between credential validation and storage writes.
threats.investigation_rationale: The external call forwards all gas and allows arbitrary code execution by the provider. While the credential state is in memory (not yet written to storage), the provider could reenter other functions that read stale storage state. The hooks contract has no reentrancy guard.

proof_of_signal.pattern: call(gas(), providerAddress, 0, ...) with no reentrancy guard; state writes happen after return in _writeLenderStatus

preconditions: Borrower must have added a malicious or compromised role provider
Provider must implement validateCredential with reentrant behavior
Target hook function must be in a state where reentrancy causes meaningful corruption

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: External CALL to untrusted provider code with full gas before state is committed to storage
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires malicious provider to be registered (borrower action)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During any deposit, transfer, or withdrawal that invokes credential validation via hooksData

operational_questions: Can a malicious provider reenter the hooks to grant itself additional credentials?
Can reentrancy allow bypassing the credential replacement rules in _grantRole?
Does the market contract have reentrancy protection that limits the impact?
validation_requirements: Deploy malicious provider that reenters hooks on validateCredential callback
Test specific reentrancy paths: grantRole, blockFromDeposits, onDeposit
Verify if market-level reentrancy guards mitigate the risk

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Borrower registers malicious provider via addRoleProvider
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Provider's validateCredential makes reentrant calls to the hooks contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: During reentrant call, provider calls grantRole to set credentials before outer call completes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Outer _writeLenderStatus may overwrite the reentrantly-set credential with stale data

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Provider can manipulate credential state for arbitrary lenders during callbacks
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Stale credential state written to storage after reentrant modifications
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires provider removal and credential re-issuance

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unusual call depth during credential validation
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Provider making calls back to hooks during validation

threats.title: Gas Griefing via Malicious Pull Provider Consuming All Gas in Credential Loop
threats.hypothesis_description: Both _tryGetCredential and _loopTryGetCredential pass all remaining gas to external pull providers via `staticcall(gas(), providerAddress, ...)`. A malicious or buggy pull provider can consume all available gas (e.g., infinite loop), causing the entire credential validation to revert with Out of Gas. Since _loopTryGetCredential iterates over all pull providers, even a single malicious provider can DoS all credential validation, preventing deposits, transfers, and withdrawals for all lenders on all markets using the hooks instance.
threats.investigation_rationale: The gas() opcode forwards all remaining gas to the staticcall. There is no gas limit cap for the external call. A single malicious provider in the _pullProviders array blocks all credential refresh attempts since the loop encounters the OOG revert.

proof_of_signal.pattern: staticcall(gas(), providerAddress, ...) forwards all remaining gas; no gas cap; failure propagates as OOG to parent

preconditions: A pull provider must be registered that consumes excessive gas (malicious or buggy)
The lender's credential validation must reach the loop path (no valid cached credential)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: All gas forwarded to external calls with no cap; single provider failure DoSes entire loop
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Borrower must have added the malicious provider (or provider was later compromised)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent until borrower removes the malicious provider

operational_questions: Does the staticcall's failure propagate as an OOG or as a silent false return?
Can the borrower remove the malicious provider quickly enough to restore service?
Is there a gas limit that would prevent the loop from being fully DoSed?
validation_requirements: Deploy a gas-consuming mock pull provider
Register it and verify credential validation fails with OOG
Confirm borrower can remove provider to restore service

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromise or deploy a malicious pull provider with gas-consuming getCredential
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Borrower unknowingly adds it via addRoleProvider
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All credential validation attempts that reach the loop fail with OOG
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Deposits, transfers, withdrawals blocked for all lenders until provider is removed
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Provider deployment and social engineering of borrower

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete market freeze for deposits and withdrawals
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Borrower must identify and remove the malicious provider
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: onDeposit, onTransfer, onQueueWithdrawal on all markets

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium - requires provider to be registered

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: OOG reverts on credential validation transactions
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Consistent OOG at a specific call depth

threats.title: HooksConfig.onBorrow Uses RepayHook_Base_Size Instead of BorrowHook_Base_Size Constant
threats.hypothesis_description: In HooksConfig.sol's `onBorrow` function, the assembly block calculates the hook call size using `add(RepayHook_Base_Size, extraCalldataBytes)` instead of `add(BorrowHook_Base_Size, extraCalldataBytes)`. Both constants are currently defined as 0x0224, so there is no functional impact. However, this is a code defect where the wrong constant is referenced. If either constant is modified independently in a future update, the borrow hook would send incorrectly-sized calldata to the hooks contract, potentially truncating or padding the call data and causing the hooks contract to revert or misinterpret parameters.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function clearly handles borrow operations but references a repay-related constant. The constant values happen to be identical (0x0224) because borrow and repay hooks have the same calldata structure, but using the wrong named constant creates a maintenance hazard.

proof_of_signal.pattern: let size := add(RepayHook_Base_Size, extraCalldataBytes) — should be BorrowHook_Base_Size

preconditions: Currently no impact since both constants equal 0x0224
Risk manifests only if constants are changed independently in future

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Wrong constant name in borrow function referencing repay constant

operational_questions: Are BorrowHook_Base_Size and RepayHook_Base_Size always identical by design?
Should the constants be unified into one if they always match?
Are there other hook functions with similar constant misreferences?
validation_requirements: Verify both constants are identical: BorrowHook_Base_Size == RepayHook_Base_Size == 0x0224
Confirm no functional impact in current deployment
Flag for code review correction

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If constants diverge in future update, onBorrow sends wrong calldata size
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Hooks contract receives truncated/malformed data, potentially reverting or misinterpreting
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All borrow operations would fail or behave incorrectly

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Potential future borrow operation failures if constants diverge

threats.title: MarketState.withdrawableProtocolFees Underflow Blocks Fee Collection in Undercollateralized Markets
threats.hypothesis_description: In MarketState.sol's `withdrawableProtocolFees`, the calculation `uint256 totalAvailableAssets = totalAssets - state.normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals` will revert with an arithmetic underflow in Solidity 0.8+ if `totalAssets < state.normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals`. This can occur when a market becomes severely undercollateralized (e.g., borrower defaults, bad debt accumulates). The revert would prevent the protocol from collecting any fees and could block critical market functions that call this method.
threats.investigation_rationale: Solidity 0.8.25 (configured in foundry.toml) has checked arithmetic by default. The subtraction has no explicit underflow protection (no satSub). If market becomes insolvent, this function reverts instead of returning 0, potentially bricking fee collection paths.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 totalAvailableAssets = totalAssets - state.normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals; unchecked subtraction in checked arithmetic context

preconditions: Market must be severely undercollateralized: totalAssets < normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals
This typically occurs during borrower default or bad debt scenarios

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unchecked subtraction that reverts instead of returning 0 in edge case

operational_questions: Is withdrawableProtocolFees called in state-changing market functions (not just views)?
Does the revert block critical operations like withdrawal processing?
Should the function use satSub and return 0 in undercollateralized scenarios?
validation_requirements: Create a market state where totalAssets < normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals
Call withdrawableProtocolFees and verify it reverts
Trace callers to determine impact on critical paths

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Borrower defaults, totalAssets drops below normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Protocol fee collection and potentially other market operations are blocked
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Market enters a degraded state where certain operations are permanently blocked

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Protocol fee collection blocked in insolvent markets
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Market functions dependent on this calculation revert
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Protocol fee withdrawal, potentially market state updates

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverting fee collection transactions on distressed markets
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Inability to process fees during default scenarios
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Market operations failing when totalAssets drops

threats.title: Borrower Can Instantly Eliminate Fixed-Term Lockup by Setting fixedTermEndTime to Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: FixedTermLoanHooks.setFixedTermEndTime allows the borrower to set the fixed term end time to 0 (or any past timestamp). The only check is `newFixedTermEndTime > hookedMarket.fixedTermEndTime` (must not increase). Since 0 is always <= any existing value, the borrower can instantly eliminate the fixed-term lockup protection. In onQueueWithdrawal, the check `market.fixedTermEndTime > block.timestamp` becomes `0 > block.timestamp` which is always false, allowing immediate withdrawals. This breaks the fixed-term guarantee that lenders relied upon when depositing.
threats.investigation_rationale: The setFixedTermEndTime function enforces monotonic decrease but has no minimum value check. The borrower could attract deposits with a long fixed term, then set it to 0, allowing themselves to trigger mass withdrawals or remove the liquidity guarantee.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Only checks newFixedTermEndTime > hookedMarket.fixedTermEndTime (no increase), no minimum value check; 0 is always valid

preconditions: Borrower has deployed a fixed-term market
Borrower calls setFixedTermEndTime with newFixedTermEndTime = 0

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No minimum value enforcement in setFixedTermEndTime
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires borrower action (intended admin power, but no safeguard against eliminating the term entirely)

operational_questions: Is eliminating the fixed term entirely an intended borrower capability?
Should there be a minimum remaining term or should the function only allow reduction to current block.timestamp?
Do lenders have any on-chain recourse if the term is eliminated?
validation_requirements: Call setFixedTermEndTime(market, 0) as borrower
Verify lenders can immediately queue withdrawals afterward
Assess impact on lender expectations and market stability

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Borrower creates market with attractive fixed term (e.g., 180 days)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Lenders deposit based on the term commitment
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Borrower calls setFixedTermEndTime(market, 0), instantly eliminating the lockup
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Borrower can now borrow without the liquidity guarantee that the fixed term provided

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Market model breaks - borrower loses fixed-term liquidity assurance
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot restore the fixed term once reduced

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: setFixedTermEndTime called with value 0 or far-past timestamp
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Term reduction shortly after large deposit inflow

threats.title: Credential Cross-Contamination: Global _lenderStatus Shared Across All Markets on Same Hooks Instance
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_lenderStatus` mapping is global across all markets using the same hooks instance. A credential granted by any role provider automatically applies to ALL markets managed by the same hooks contract, not just the intended market. While `isKnownLenderOnMarket` is per-market (correctly), the initial credential check in `_tryValidateAccessInner` uses the global `_lenderStatus`. A lender approved for one market (e.g., a low-risk market) automatically has deposit access to all other markets (e.g., high-risk markets) managed by the same hooks instance, bypassing any intended per-market access differentiation.
threats.investigation_rationale: _lenderStatus[account] stores a single credential that is checked by all market hooks. The hooks instance is deployed per-borrower and shared across markets. If the borrower intends different access policies per market (e.g., different risk tiers), the shared credential makes this impossible.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _lenderStatus[account] is a single global mapping used by all markets; no market-specific credential scoping

preconditions: Borrower deploys multiple markets through the same hooks instance
Borrower intends different access control policies per market
A lender obtains a credential for any one market

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Single _lenderStatus mapping shared across all markets; credential from market A grants access to markets B, C, etc.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Borrower configuration of multiple markets on same hooks

operational_questions: Is per-market access differentiation an expected use case for borrowers with multiple markets?
Should _lenderStatus be scoped per-market instead of global?
Can this lead to compliance violations if different markets have different KYC requirements?
validation_requirements: Deploy two markets with same hooks instance
Grant credential for market A only
Verify lender can deposit in market B without separate approval

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain credential for the least restrictive market under a borrower's hooks instance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Use the same credential to deposit in more restrictive markets
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Bypass any intended per-market access control differentiation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Access restricted markets without meeting their specific credential requirements

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Per-market access control policy cannot be enforced
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires separate hooks instances per market (architectural change)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All credential-gated operations across all markets on the same hooks

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After obtaining credential for any market

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Deposits from lenders who only have credentials for different markets
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Lenders accessing markets they shouldn't have credentials for
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Credential obtained for easy market, deposits made in restricted market
